  description: "2.1 Automatic Differentiation in PyTorch: From Forward Computation to Backpropagation"
title: "2.1 Automatic Differentiation in PyTorch: From Forward Computation to Backpropagation"
order: 1
---

In Section 1.3, we understood backpropagation through computation graphs: a loss changes because it depends on a chain of earlier computations, and walking backward along that chain tells us how much responsibility each parameter carries.

Here we revisit the same idea in PyTorch. During the forward pass, PyTorch records how tensors are produced; during the backward pass, it follows those recorded operations and applies each operation's local derivative rule.

The examples below use small tensors so that the bookkeeping behind autograd is visible. Code blocks and formulas are kept exactly as in the original notebook.

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

::: {#d2a26553 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import warnings

import torch
import torch.autograd.functional as AF
from torch import Tensor

print('PyTorch version:', torch.__version__)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
PyTorch version: 2.12.0+xpu
```
:::
:::


## 2.1.1 The Computation Graph Is Built at Runtime

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

$$

z = \sin(x \cdot y)

$$

::: {#93619d64 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0, requires_grad=True)
```
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

::: {#4b6390c9 .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
q = x.dot(y)
z = q.sin()
print('z.requires_grad:', z.requires_grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
z.requires_grad: True
```
:::
:::


The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

::: {#4d66212d .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
print('z.grad_fn:', z.grad_fn.name())
print('q.grad_fn:', q.grad_fn.name())
print('x.grad_fn:', x.grad_fn)
print('y.grad_fn:', y.grad_fn)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
z.grad_fn: SinBackward0
q.grad_fn: DotBackward0
x.grad_fn: None
y.grad_fn: None
```
:::
:::


These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

::: {#ca37b5d3 .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
print('x.grad:', x.grad)
print('y.grad:', y.grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
x.grad: None
y.grad: None
```
:::
:::


This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

## 2.1.2 backward(): Propagating Gradients Back from the Output

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

$$

z = \sin(q), \quad q = x \cdot y

$$

$$

\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}

= \frac{\partial z}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q}{\partial x}

= \cos(q) \cdot y

$$

$$

\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}

= \frac{\partial z}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q}{\partial y}

= \cos(q) \cdot x

$$

::: {#425ca35a .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
z.backward()
```
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

::: {#0fd561e7 .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
print('x.grad:', x.grad)
print('y.grad:', y.grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
x.grad: tensor([3.1666, 3.7999, 4.4332, 5.0666])
y.grad: tensor([0.6333, 1.2666, 1.9000, 2.5333])
```
:::
:::


The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

::: {#c4b5b721 .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
expected_x_grad = y * x.dot(y).cos()
expected_y_grad = x * x.dot(y).cos()

assert torch.allclose(x.grad, expected_x_grad)
assert torch.allclose(y.grad, expected_y_grad)
```
:::


These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

::: {#a8f208d8 .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
node_q = z.grad_fn.next_functions[0][0]
node_x = node_q.next_functions[0][0]
node_y = node_q.next_functions[1][0]

print('z ->', z.grad_fn.name())
print('q ->', node_q.name())
print('x ->', node_x.name())
print('y ->', node_y.name())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
z -> SinBackward0
q -> DotBackward0
x -> struct torch::autograd::AccumulateGrad
y -> struct torch::autograd::AccumulateGrad
```
:::
:::


This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

## 2.1.3 Why Non-Scalar Outputs Cannot Call backward() Directly

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

::: {#6178510d .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0, requires_grad=True)
Z = x.outer(y)

try:
    Z.backward()
except RuntimeError as err:
    print('RuntimeError:', err)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
RuntimeError: grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs
```
:::
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

$$

\frac{\partial z}{\partial z} = 1

$$

::: {#256dc33c .cell execution_count=11}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0, requires_grad=True)

Z = x.outer(y)
Z.backward(gradient=torch.ones_like(Z))

print('x.grad:', x.grad)
print('y.grad:', y.grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
x.grad: tensor([26., 26., 26., 26.])
y.grad: tensor([10., 10., 10., 10.])
```
:::
:::


The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

::: {#0c774356 .cell execution_count=12}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0, requires_grad=True)

Z = x.outer(y)
loss = Z.sum()
loss.backward()

print('x.grad:', x.grad)
print('y.grad:', y.grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
x.grad: tensor([26., 26., 26., 26.])
y.grad: tensor([10., 10., 10., 10.])
```
:::
:::


These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

## 2.1.4 Higher-Order Derivatives: Making Differentiation Part of the Graph

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

::: {#b3f91793 .cell execution_count=13}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.tensor(4.0, requires_grad=True)
z = torch.sin(x * y)

dzdx, dzdy = torch.autograd.grad(z, (x, y), create_graph=True)

print('dz/dx:', dzdx)
print('dz/dy:', dzdy)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
dz/dx: tensor(-0.5820, grad_fn=<MulBackward0>)
dz/dy: tensor(-0.2910, grad_fn=<MulBackward0>)
```
:::
:::


The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

::: {#d693b235 .cell execution_count=14}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.tensor(4.0, requires_grad=True)
z = torch.sin(x * y)

dzdx, dzdy = torch.autograd.grad(z, (x, y), create_graph=True)
print('dz/dx:', dzdx)
print('dz/dy:', dzdy)

(d2zdx2,) = torch.autograd.grad(dzdx, x, retain_graph=True)
(d2zdy2,) = torch.autograd.grad(dzdy, y)
print('d2z/dx2:', d2zdx2)
print('d2z/dy2:', d2zdy2)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
dz/dx: tensor(-0.5820, grad_fn=<MulBackward0>)
dz/dy: tensor(-0.2910, grad_fn=<MulBackward0>)
d2z/dx2: tensor(-15.8297)
d2z/dy2: tensor(-3.9574)
```
:::
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

## 2.1.6 VJP and JVP: Autograd Usually Avoids the Full Jacobian

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

$$

f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m

$$

$$

J = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}

$$

### 2.1.6.1 VJP: Vector-Jacobian Product

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

$$

v^\top J \in \mathbb{R}^n

$$

$$

L = \mathcal{L}(f(x))

$$

$$

\frac{\partial L}{\partial f}

$$

$$

\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} =

\frac{\partial L}{\partial f}

\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}

$$

::: {#5820c32a .cell execution_count=15}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def vjp_func(x: Tensor, y: Tensor):
    return x.dot(y).sin()


x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0)
output = AF.vjp(vjp_func, (x, y))

print('func(x,y):', output[0])
print('VJP output:', output[1])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
func(x,y): tensor(0.7739)
VJP output: (tensor([3.1666, 3.7999, 4.4332, 5.0666]), tensor([0.6333, 1.2666, 1.9000, 2.5333]))
```
:::
:::


This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

### 2.1.6.2 JVP: Jacobian-Vector Product

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

$$

Ju \in \mathbb{R}^m

$$

::: {#07791806 .cell execution_count=16}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def jvp_func(x: Tensor, y: Tensor):
    return x.dot(y).sin()


x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0)
u_x = torch.full_like(x, 0.1)
u_y = torch.full_like(y, 0.2)
output = AF.jvp(jvp_func, (x, y), (u_x, u_y))

print('func(x,y):', output[0])
print('JVP output:', output[1])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
func(x,y): tensor(0.7739)
JVP output: tensor(2.9133)
```
:::
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

## 2.1.7 Common Issues in Backpropagation

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

::: {#2d48532b .cell execution_count=17}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.arange(1.0, 5.0, requires_grad=True)
y = torch.arange(5.0, 9.0, requires_grad=True)
```
:::


#### 1. Calling backward() Repeatedly

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

::: {#1388270b .cell execution_count=18}
``` {.python .cell-code}
z = x.dot(y).sin()
z.backward()

try:
    z.backward()
except RuntimeError as err:
    print('RuntimeError:', err)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
RuntimeError: Trying to backward through the graph a second time (or directly access saved tensors after they have already been freed). Saved intermediate values of the graph are freed when you call .backward() or autograd.grad(). Specify retain_graph=True if you need to backward through the graph a second time or if you need to access saved tensors after calling backward.
```
:::
:::


This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

::: {#79410bcc .cell execution_count=19}
``` {.python .cell-code}
z = x.dot(y).sin()
z.backward(retain_graph=True)
z.backward()  # This works because we retained the graph
```
:::


#### 2. Gradients Accumulate

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

::: {#327bfa5e .cell execution_count=20}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x.grad = None  # Clear the gradient before starting
print('Before backward:', x.grad)

z1 = x.dot(y)
z1.backward()
print('After first backward:', x.grad)

z2 = x.dot(y)
z2.backward()
print('After second backward:', x.grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Before backward: None
After first backward: tensor([5., 6., 7., 8.])
After second backward: tensor([10., 12., 14., 16.])
```
:::
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

#### 3. Intermediate Nodes Do Not Store Gradients by Default

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

::: {#f35c018b .cell execution_count=21}
``` {.python .cell-code}
q = x.dot(y)
z = q.sin()
z.backward()

with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as warns:
    print('q.grad:', q.grad)
    if len(warns) > 0:
        for warn in warns:
            print('UserWarning:', warn.message)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
q.grad: None
UserWarning: The .grad attribute of a Tensor that is not a leaf Tensor is being accessed. Its .grad attribute won't be populated during autograd.backward(). If you indeed want the .grad field to be populated for a non-leaf Tensor, use .retain_grad() on the non-leaf Tensor. If you access the non-leaf Tensor by mistake, make sure you access the leaf Tensor instead. See github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/30531 for more information. (Triggered internally at C:\actions-runner\_work\pytorch\pytorch\build\aten\src\ATen/core/TensorBody.h:499.)
```
:::
:::


These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

::: {#f4c3204e .cell execution_count=22}
``` {.python .cell-code}
q = x.dot(y)
q.retain_grad()

z = q.sin()
z.backward()

print('q.grad:', q.grad)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
q.grad: tensor(0.6333)
```
:::
:::


This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

#### 4. In-Place Operations Can Break Backpropagation

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

::: {#2baf42c5 .cell execution_count=23}
``` {.python .cell-code}
z = x.dot(y)

try:
    x.relu_()
except RuntimeError as err:
    print('RuntimeError:', err)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
RuntimeError: a leaf Variable that requires grad is being used in an in-place operation.
```
:::
:::


In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

::: {#fdda4bcd .cell execution_count=24}
``` {.python .cell-code}
z = x.dot(y)
x = x.relu()
z.backward()
```
:::


## 2.1.8 Summary

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.
